item.title: One Nation secures Bainimarama’s endorsement ahead of elections
item.description: The newly registered One Nation party has announced that former Prime Minister Frank Bainimarama has formally endorsed the party and pledged his support for its vision and principles ahead of the next General Election. In a statement, One Nation President Tupou Draunidalo said a party delegation met with Bainimarama on Thursday, 2 July, to present the party’s constitution and seek his patronage an
item.content:encoded:
The newly registered One Nation party has announced that former Prime Minister Frank Bainimarama has formally endorsed the party and pledged his support for its vision and principles ahead of the next General Election.

In a statement, One Nation President Tupou Draunidalo said a party delegation met with Bainimarama on Thursday, 2 July, to present the party’s constitution and seek his patronage and support.

The delegation included the party’s president, General Secretary Ravindran Kumaran, founding members and members of the Central Executive Committee.

Draunidalo said the meeting reaffirmed the party’s commitment to building “a united, inclusive and prosperous Fiji founded on integrity, equality, meritocracy and good governance.”

General Secretary Ravindran Kumaran outlined the party’s vision, objectives and guiding principles before Draunidalo formally presented the One Nation Constitution to Bainimarama.

According to the party, Bainimarama said he recognised similarities between the values contained in the One Nation Constitution and those he championed while serving as Prime Minister.

He also expressed support for the party’s commitment to national unity, equal citizenry, equal opportunities, good governance, accountability and putting Fiji and Fijians first.

“One Nation is honoured to announce that the former Prime Minister of Fiji, Mr Frank Voreqe Bainimarama, has decided to formally endorse and pledge his support for One Nation.”

She said the endorsement represented “an important milestone” for the party as it prepares to engage with voters across the country ahead of the next General Election.

One Nation said it remains committed to serving all Fijians regardless of ethnicity, religion, province, socio-economic status, gender or background.

The party said its mission is to build a stronger and more inclusive Fiji through integrity, the rule of law, sustainable economic growth, socio-economic stability, equal opportunities, an improved business and investment environment, and responsible economic management.

One Nation also thanked Bainimarama for his support and said it looks forward to working with him while continuing to engage communities throughout Fiji and develop practical solutions to the challenges facing ordinary citizens.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Parliament Sugar Committee to visit Western cane belt this week
item.description: A parliamentary committee tasked with addressing challenges in Fiji’s sugar industry will travel to the Western Division this week to hear directly from farmers, millers and other key stakeholders as efforts continue to revive the sector. The Special Committee on the Sugar Industry, which met at Parliament on Friday, said restoring confidence among cane farmers remains a key priority, with members
item.content:encoded:
A parliamentary committee tasked with addressing challenges in Fiji’s sugar industry will travel to the Western Division this week to hear directly from farmers, millers and other key stakeholders as efforts continue to revive the sector.

The Special Committee on the Sugar Industry, which met at Parliament on Friday, said restoring confidence among cane farmers remains a key priority, with members stressing the need for clear communication about government assistance already available to growers.

The committee announced it will conduct consultations in the Western Division on Wednesday, 8 July, and Thursday, 9 July, providing farmers and industry stakeholders with an opportunity to raise concerns and suggest ways to improve the long-term viability and sustainability of the sugar industry.

The visit comes as the committee confirmed that cane harvesting is continuing in both the Western and Northern divisions.

Members also referred to a letter from National Farmers Union General Secretary Mahendra Chaudhry to Prime Minister Sitiveni Rabuka, in which he stated that “cane farmers are not on strike.”

As part of measures aimed at encouraging harvesting, the committee said the Government will increase the subsidy paid to manual harvesters from $3 to $5 per tonne to recognise their contribution and boost harvesting activity.

The committee also addressed concerns raised by farmers over the security of their standing cane crops. It said it had consulted with the Ministry of Policing for the Fiji Police to increase patrols throughout the cane belt to provide reassurance and protection for farmers who may fear intimidation or retaliation.

The committee said discussions will continue beyond this week’s consultations, with further meetings planned to examine the future direction of the sugar industry, including opportunities for diversification.

The Government also acknowledged the continued cooperation and contribution of all stakeholders involved in the sugar industry.

The Sugar Sugar Committee is chaired by Prime Minister, Sitiveni Rabuka.

Members include Professor Biman Prasad, Deputy Prime Minister and Minister for Civil Aviation and Tourism Viliame Gavoka, Manoa Kamikamica, Minister for Sugar and Multi-Ethnic Affairs Tomasi Tunabuna, Leader of Opposition Inia Seruiratu, a member designated by the Leader of the Opposition, and Minister for Policing Ioane Naivalurua.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Indian Police allege Fiji business dispute behind India shooting
item.description: A garment trader who was seriously wounded in a shooting in India’s Gujarat state was allegedly targeted over a long-running business dispute originating in Fiji, according to Indian police. The Times of India reports that Vadodara Police have arrested two men from Chhattisgarh, alleging the attack was a contract killing planned after weeks of surveillance.
item.content:encoded:
A garment trader who was seriously wounded in a shooting in India’s Gujarat state was allegedly targeted over a long-running business dispute originating in Fiji, according to Indian police.

The Times of India reports that Vadodara Police have arrested two men from Chhattisgarh, alleging the attack was a contract killing planned after weeks of surveillance.

The victim, 37-year-old was shot in the shoulder near Waghodia Chokdi on the night of June 26 while returning home from his garment shop. He remains seriously injured.

Vadodara Range Inspector General of Police Sandeep Singh said investigators examined more than 2,000 CCTV recordings and followed human and technical leads to identify the suspects and uncover the alleged conspiracy.

Police claim the two accused travelled to Vadodara on multiple occasions during June to monitor Bakoliya’s movements before carrying out the attack.

According to investigators, the victim had previously worked as a financial manager for a company in Fiji and was involved in a long-standing business dispute while employed there.

The Times of India and other media organisations have named the company and the Fiji businessman.

Police allege the dispute was the motive behind the shooting.

Authorities also claim the operation was funded through bank transfers covering travel, accommodation and weapons procurement. Investigators estimate that about FJD$5,900 (approximately ₹272,000) was transferred to support the alleged plot.

Vadodara Rural Superintendent of Police Sushil Agrawal said the investigation, conducted under Operation Parakram, involved 10 police teams from the Local Crime Branch, Special Operations Group and local police stations.

Police allege one suspect drove the motorcycle while the other fired the shot. The pair allegedly wore helmets and face coverings to conceal their identities.

Investigators recovered a pistol, two magazines and 10 rounds of ammunition believed to have been used in the attack.

One of the accused also allegedly had travel booked to Fiji on July 7, prompting police to issue a lookout notice to prevent him from leaving India.

Indian police say the investigation is continuing and emphasised that allegations regarding the motive, financial transactions and any other individuals linked to the case remain part of an ongoing investigation.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Westpac questions long-term future of Fiji’s sugar industry
item.description: Westpac says Fiji faces a difficult decision over the long-term future of its sugar industry, warning that continuing to invest heavily in the sector may not be sustainable without major reforms and a gradual transition to alternative livelihoods. In its Fiji FY2026-27 National Budget Review, Westpac said Fiji’s sugar industry has become increasingly uncompetitive since losing preferential Europea
item.content:encoded:
Westpac says Fiji faces a difficult decision over the long-term future of its sugar industry, warning that continuing to invest heavily in the sector may not be sustainable without major reforms and a gradual transition to alternative livelihoods.

In its Fiji FY2026-27 National Budget Review, Westpac said Fiji’s sugar industry has become increasingly uncompetitive since losing preferential European Union sugar prices.

“Fiji no longer benefits from the preferential prices once offered by the European Union, which at one stage paid nearly three times the prevailing world market price for sugar.”

The report says Fiji now competes with major sugar-producing countries such as India, Brazil and Australia, all of which enjoy significant competitive advantages.

“Today, Fiji must compete against large-scale producers such as India and Brazil, which benefit from lower labour costs, greater economies of scale, larger state subsidies and more advanced technology.”

Westpac noted that Fiji has restructured its sugar industry before, with mills opening and closing over time as economic conditions changed.

“The big question is whether Fiji should keep directing money into sugar or help farmers shift to other livelihoods.”

The report said mechanisation alone has not solved the industry’s challenges, particularly in hilly areas where harvesting remains difficult.

It also warned that mechanically harvested cane contains more “extraneous matter,” reducing sugar yields because Fiji’s ageing mills were designed primarily to process manually harvested cane.

Westpac said small farm sizes, long distances from mills and ports, and continued dependence on manual labour have further undermined the industry’s competitiveness.

Despite these challenges, the bank said the industry could not simply be shut down because thousands of families still depend on it.

“Fiji cannot shut the industry overnight because 10,000 growers depend on it, that is about one percent of the population, or four percent if you count family members.”

Instead, Westpac recommended a gradual, long-term transition.

“Reforms are needed to assist existing farmers and industry participants to gradually transition to alternative income sources.”

While describing mechanisation as essential to improving profitability, the report said achieving that goal would require substantial capital investment that Fiji currently lacks.

“Such transition will require a gradual long-term roadmap.”

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Election bodies back coup candidate ban
item.description: The Electoral Commission and the Fijian Elections Office have recommended that people who rely on constitutional immunity for coup-related actions should be permanently barred from contesting elections. In their joint submission to the Constitution Review Commission, the Commissioners said candidates benefiting from coup-related constitutional immunity should not be eligible to stand for public of
item.content:encoded:
The Electoral Commission and the Fijian Elections Office have recommended that people who rely on constitutional immunity for coup-related actions should be permanently barred from contesting elections.

In their joint submission to the Constitution Review Commission, the Commissioners said candidates benefiting from coup-related constitutional immunity should not be eligible to stand for public office.

“The Commissioners recommended that any person who relies on coup-related constitutional immunity be permanently disqualified from standing as a candidate.”

However, the submission acknowledges that the Electoral Commission does not have the authority to make such a change and says the matter must be decided through constitutional reform.

“The Commission considers this question to fall outside its administrative mandate and defers to the Commission of Review.”

The submission also notes that the Supreme Court, in its 2025 advisory opinion, deliberately left the constitutional immunity provisions untouched.

“It notes that the Supreme Court, in its 2025 advisory opinion, declined to disturb the immunity and transitional provisions preserved by the Constitution.”

The Commission says the interaction between candidate eligibility and constitutional immunity is ultimately a matter for constitutional policymakers.

“The interaction between candidate disqualification and those provisions is a matter of constitutional policy for the Commission of Review and Parliament.”

The recommendation forms part of the Electoral Commission and the Fijian Elections Office’s submissions to the Constitution Review Commission as it considers possible reforms to Fiji’s constitutional framework.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Albanese arrives in Fiji to strengthen Vuvale Partnership
item.description: Australian Prime Minister Anthony Albanese arrived in Fiji this evening for an official visit aimed at reinforcing the Fiji–Australia Vuvale Partnership and advancing cooperation on shared regional priorities. Mr Albanese was welcomed at Nausori Airport by Deputy Prime Minister Viliame Gavoka and Minister for Foreign Affairs and External Trade Sakiasi Ditoka.
item.content:encoded:
Australian Prime Minister Anthony Albanese arrived in Fiji this evening for an official visit aimed at reinforcing the Fiji–Australia Vuvale Partnership and advancing cooperation on shared regional priorities.

Mr Albanese was welcomed at Nausori Airport by Deputy Prime Minister Viliame Gavoka and Minister for Foreign Affairs and External Trade Sakiasi Ditoka.

He was accorded a 50-member Guard of Honour by the Republic of Fiji Military Forces.

Accompanying the Australian Prime Minister is Australia’s Minister for Foreign Affairs, Senator Penny Wong.

During the visit, Mr Albanese is scheduled to participate in a traditional welcoming ceremony, hold bilateral talks with the Fiji Government, and pay a courtesy call on President Ratu Naiqama Lalabalavu tomorrow morning.

In a statement, the Fiji Government said the visit reflects the enduring friendship between Fiji and Australia and reaffirms both nations’ commitment to the Vuvale Partnership, which is built on mutual respect, cooperation and a shared vision for a peaceful, secure and prosperous Pacific region.

The visit is expected to provide an opportunity for both countries to strengthen collaboration on issues including regional security, climate resilience, economic development and people-to-people ties.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Widow battles cane fires
item.description: WHILE many farmers have the option to delay harvesting their cane farms, a grieving widow, Naseem Begum, says she is struggling to survive amid rising field fires and a lack of police support. Ms Begum, a Karavi, Ba resident, is facing her first harvest alone following the death of her husband three months ago.
item.content:encoded:
WHILE many farmers have the option to delay harvesting their cane farms, a grieving widow, Naseem Begum, says she is struggling to survive amid rising field fires and a lack of police support.

Ms Begum, a Karavi, Ba resident, is facing her first harvest alone following the death of her husband three months ago.

She claims her crop has been targeted by local youths who have deliberately set cane fields alight.

Despite the setback, Ms Begum began harvesting this week using a mechanical harvester.

She said she was unfamiliar with the financial arrangements previously managed by her late husband, including costs for harvesting and lorry transport, but remains determined to complete the harvest.

Last year, the farm produced 228 tonnes of cane.

This year, despite the fires, she hopes to salvage at least 100 tonnes from the remaining crop near the Kings Highway.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Trio held over robbery
item.description: THREE men have been arrested in connection with an alleged robbery in Cunningham, Suva, on Friday morning, with police investigating possible links to other robberies in the area. The incident, captured on CCTV, prompted investigations by officers at Valelevu Police Station in Nasinu, leading to the arrest of a main suspect and two alleged accomplices.
item.content:encoded:
THREE men have been arrested in connection with an alleged robbery in Cunningham, Suva, on Friday morning, with police investigating possible links to other robberies in the area.

The incident, captured on CCTV, prompted investigations by officers at Valelevu Police Station in Nasinu, leading to the arrest of a main suspect and two alleged accomplices.

“The suspect alleged to be involved in a robbery case in Cunningham on Friday morning has been arrested by Valelevu police.

“Investigations into the initial report of robbery led to information on the involvement of two others, who have also been arrested,” police said in a statement.

Police said the three suspects are also allegedly linked to other robbery cases in the area.

The trio remain in custody at Valelevu Police Station as investigations continue.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.
